subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Chain Rule, Product Rule & FTC with Table of Values
stimulus:
The functions $f$ and $g$ are twice differentiable. The table shown gives values of the functions and their first derivatives at selected values of $x$.

| $x$ | 0 | 2 | 4 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f(x)$ | 10 | 7 | 4 | 5 |
| $f'(x)$ | $\frac{3}{2}$ | $-8$ | 3 | 6 |
| $g(x)$ | 1 | 2 | $-3$ | 0 |
| $g'(x)$ | 5 | 4 | 2 | 8 |

parts.prompt: Let $h$ be the function defined by $h(x) = f(g(x))$. Find $h'(7)$. Show the work that leads to your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Applies chain rule: $h'(x) = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$
1 pt: Correct answer: $h'(7) = f'(g(7)) \cdot g'(7) = f'(0) \cdot 8 = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 8 = 12$
parts.scoring_notes: Chain rule form earns first point. Answer of $12$ earns second point only if chain rule was applied. Without chain rule setup, second point requires showing $f'(0) \cdot 8 = 12$ or $\frac{3}{2} \cdot 8$. Unsupported answer of $12$ earns no points.

parts.prompt: Let $k$ be a differentiable function such that $k'(x) = (f(x))^2 \cdot g(x)$. Is the graph of $k$ concave up or concave down at the point where $x = 4$? Give a reason for your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Applies product rule (and chain rule): $k''(x) = 2f(x) \cdot f'(x) \cdot g(x) + (f(x))^2 \cdot g'(x)$
parts.scoring_notes: First point earned for correct differentiation form (product + chain rule). A single error in applying product or chain rule still earns first point, but prevents second point. Third point consistent with any declared nonzero value of $k''(4)$.

parts.prompt: Let $m$ be the function defined by $m(x) = 5x^3 + \int_0^x f'(t)\, dt$. Find $m(2)$. Show the work that leads to your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct answer with supporting work: $m(2) = 5(8) + \int_0^2 f'(t)\, dt = 40 + (f(2) - f(0)) = 40 + (7 - 10) = 37$
parts.scoring_notes: Must show supporting work equivalent to $5 \cdot 8 + (f(2) - f(0))$ or $40 + (7 - 10)$. Unsupported answer of $37$ earns no credit. Uses FTC: $\int_0^2 f'(t)\, dt = f(2) - f(0) = 7 - 10 = -3$.

parts.prompt: Is the function $m$ defined in part (c) increasing, decreasing, or neither at $x = 2$? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: First point earned for considering $m'$. Second point requires $m'(2) = 60 + f'(2)$ or $60 + (-8)$ with work; unsupported $m'(2) = 52$ does not earn. Third point consistent with any declared value of $m'(2)$.
